retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional approach is mandatory for the required outcomes in the management of PPH, including: Obstetrics and gynecology Maternal-fetal medicine General surgery Anesthesiology Hematology Urology Emergency medicine Laboratory personnel Interventional radiology

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- General Postpartum Hemorrhage Management
retrieved_docs.content: The approach to treating PPH is focused on the patient's resuscitation when identifying and treating the underlying cause. Maintaining the patient's hemodynamic stability ensures continued perfusion to vital organs. As soon as PPH is identified, 2 large bore peripheral intravenous (IV) catheters (14- or 16G) should be placed, and maternal blood type and antibody screen should be confirmed in anticipation of aggressive corrective measures. Direct assessment of cumulative blood loss is essential, and a focus on early initiation of protocols for releasing blood products and massive transfusion protocols is necessary. Crystalloid and colloid IV fluids should be administered as indicated, and clinicians should also evaluate patients to identify the underlying cause and tailor treatments. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate the etiologies of postpartum hemorrhage based on patient history and physical exam. Identify the clinical features of postpartum hemorrhage, primarily focusing on blood loss. Apply resuscitative strategies to manage postpartum hemorrhage in pregnant individuals. Implement interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and communication, improving outcomes in patients with postpartum hemorrhage. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Post-partum hemorrhage is a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity in the United States; however, correct and timely institution of treatment can vastly improve patient outcomes. Patients who had PPH in a previous delivery are at risk of having PPH in subsequent deliveries. Implementation of standardized PPH protocols and interprofessional simulation training has helped improve patient outcomes. Additionally, increased efforts to enhance coordination among healthcare team members in response to PPH have contributed to reduced maternal morbidity. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- Additional Postpartum Hemorrhage Management Strategies
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with continued PPH due to any etiology unresponsive to initial interventions, other management options include hypogastric artery ligation, uterine artery embolization, and hysterectomy. Uterine artery embolization may be considered in a patient who is stable with persistent bleeding. Fluoroscopy is used to identify and occlude bleeding vessels. While those who are unstable are not a candidate for this modality, benefits include uterine conservation and preservation of fertility. However, studies have demonstrated that patients who have undergone uterine artery embolization have an increased risk of infertility, preterm delivery, and intrauterine growth restriction.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: PPH necessitates a cohesive interprofessional team comprising physicians, technicians, nurses, pharmacists, laboratory personnel, and labor and delivery nurses. The primary focus is prompt resuscitation while pinpointing and addressing the underlying cause, often surgical. Maintaining patient hemodynamic stability is paramount for organ perfusion. The team ensures ample intravenous access and employs direct blood loss assessment, initiating protocols for blood products and massive transfusions. Simultaneously, rapid identification of the cause and treatment initiation occurs. Throughout this process, clear communication between team members and care coordination is critical to prevent missed diagnoses and delays in intervention. Resuscitation ideally unfolds in an operating department setting, allowing anesthesia assistance for complex laceration repairs, uterine inversion correction, analgesia provision, or surgical exploration if needed. Effective communication, clear delineation of responsibilities, and coordinated efforts among team members optimize patient-centered care, enhance outcomes, ensure patient safety, and elevate team performance in managing PPH.

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Labor: Physiology, Evaluation, and Management -- Treatment / Management -- Reducing the risk of postpartum hemorrhage
retrieved_docs.content: Active management of the 3rd stage of labor involves administering uterotonics, typically oxytocin, which reduces the risk of postpartum hemorrhage and is recommended by several professional organizations. Oxytocin may be given as early as the delivery of the anterior shoulder or after the entire neonate or placenta is delivered. However, this medication should not be administered before the anterior shoulder is delivered, as it may worsen shoulder dystocia if present.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: PPH is frequently subdivided based on symptom onset. Primary PPH is hemorrhage that occurs between the third stage of labor (ie, delivery of the placenta) and 24 hours after fetal delivery; secondary PPH occurs more than 24 hours after delivery—up to 12 weeks postpartum. Causes, summarized by the 4 "T's" (tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin), require prompt intervention. Management involves a multidisciplinary approach, emphasizing blood loss assessment, fluid replacement, and source control. Despite advancements, PPH prevention and prediction remain essential to maternal well-being. PPH poses a significant challenge in obstetrics, complicated by difficulties in accurately estimating blood loss, as cognitive biases can lead to delays in diagnosis and management. Though more accurate, quantitative blood loss measurement methods have not consistently improved clinical outcomes. Interprofessional approaches, including PPH bundles and perinatal quality collaboratives, reduce morbidity. Management involves a coordinated effort addressing uterine atony, genital tract lacerations, retained placental tissue, and coagulopathy, with surgical interventions like hysterectomy as a last resort. Prevention strategies include active management of the third stage of labor and prenatal identification of high-risk factors. Vigilance and prompt intervention remain crucial in mitigating maternal morbidity and mortality associated with PPH. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- Hemodynamic Management
retrieved_docs.content: Implementing an effective blood transfusion protocol when the underlying etiology is being assessed and treated is critical to patient resuscitation and achieving hemodynamic stability. Severe hemorrhage leads to acidosis, hypothermia, and coagulopathy, which must be quickly corrected to avoid the risk of mortality. Therefore, other patient status indicators, including heart rate, respirations, peripheral oxygen saturation, temperature, and blood gases, should be monitored in addition to blood pressure. In patients with severe PPH, a massive transfusion protocol must frequently be initiated, which is standard in most institutions. Massive transfusion is defined as a transfusion of more than 10 units of packed RBCs within 24 hours or 4 RBC units in 1 hour with additional units anticipated. Most massive transfusion protocols recommend a combination of packed RBCs, fresh-frozen plasma, platelets, and cryoprecipitate in fixed ratios. The most common ratio utilized is 1:1:1 (1 unit of packed RBCs, 1 unit of fresh-frozen plasma, and 1 unit of platelets); other recommended protocols include 6:4:1 and 4:4:1.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) poses a significant risk to maternal health and is characterized by excessive blood loss after delivery. While uterine contractions and the coagulation cascade primarily regulate blood loss, PPH can lead to severe complications if left untreated. The traditional definition of PPH, based on blood loss thresholds, has evolved to encompass broader criteria of a cumulative blood loss of 1000 mL or more of blood loss associated with signs or symptoms of hypovolemia during any mode of delivery. Primary PPH occurs within 24 hours postpartum, while secondary PPH happens up to 12 weeks later. Causes, summarized by the 4 "T's" (tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin), require prompt intervention. Management involves an interprofessional approach, emphasizing accurate blood loss assessment, fluid replacement, and source control.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity for healthcare professionals is designed to enhance the learner's competence in promptly diagnosing and implementing appropriate interventions for patients with PPH to improve outcomes. Key elements include maintaining patient hemodynamic stability, ensuring ample intravenous access, assessing blood loss, initiating blood product protocols, and ensuring clear communication and coordination among healthcare team members. Resuscitation typically occurs in an operating room, allowing for comprehensive care, including anesthesia assistance for complex interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- Obstetrical Trauma
retrieved_docs.content: Rapid identification of the cause of PPH and the initiation of treatment should be simultaneous, including identifying genital lacerations. If lacerations are identified as the source of bleeding, they should be repaired quickly and bleeding should be reassessed. Transfer to an operating suite with anesthesia assistance may be indicated if uterine artery laceration is suspected. Genital tract hematomas may be suggested by clinical deterioration in the absence of other findings or genital pressure. Management of rapidly expanding hematomas includes packing, arterial embolization, or suturing. Routine antibiotic prophylaxis is not recommended. See StatPearls' companion references, " Perineal Lacerations " and " Lower Genitourinary Trauma ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- Uterine Atony Management
retrieved_docs.content: Carboprost : As a 15-methyl prostaglandin F2-α analog, carboprost acts on prostaglandin receptors to stimulate uterine contractions. The recommended dosage is 250 μg IM or intramyometrially every 15 to 90 minutes for a maximum of 8 doses, with peak serum concentrations reached in approximately 15 minutes. Carboprost is contraindicated in severe hepatic, renal, and cardiovascular disease and may cause bronchospasm in patients with asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- History and Physical -- Postpartum Hemorrhage Clinical Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Examining the patient during the hemorrhage can help identify the probable cause of bleeding focused on any specific risk factors. A rapid assessment of the entire lower genital tract, including vaginal walls, cervix, and labia, for lacerations, hematomas, or signs of uterine rupture (eg, regression of fetal presenting part and maternal abdominal tenderness), should be performed. [15] [16] The placenta should be examined to determine if the tissue remains intact, and a manual examination with extraction should be performed for any retained placental tissue; a bedside ultrasound assessment may be a part of the evaluation. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- Uterine Atony Management
retrieved_docs.content: Tranexamic acid : Tranexamic acid (TXA) is not uterotonic but inhibits fibrinolysis and is frequently used with uterotonic medications. The recommended dosage is 1 g of tranexamic acid IV over 10 minutes within 3 hours of delivery after a PPH diagnosis. TXA's onset of action is typically 5 minutes and is contraindicated in patients with a history of hypercoagulopathy. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 734
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a broad category of maternal complications that encompasses several specific conditions. It has as child entities postpartum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة), failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض), and fetal death (موت الجنين). In addition, this category is classified as obstetric cervical tear (تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم) and pelvic obstetric hematoma (ورم دموي توليدي حوضي). Together, these interrelated complications illustrate the diverse and serious events that can arise during the postpartum period and influence the clinical management of the mother.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
retrieved_docs.content: tissue disorder categories, which in turn relate to general labor‑and‑delivery care indications. The umbrella of “Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother” integrates distinct clinical outcomes—postpartum hemorrhage, fetal death, cervical laceration, and pelvic hematoma—demonstrating the interconnectedness of maternal tissue pathology, obstetric injuries, and the need for comprehensive postpartum management. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in linking anatomical abnormalities to potential obstetric complications and in selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies during labor, delivery, and the puerperium.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 773
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a broad obstetric category that encompasses specific child complications such as نزف ما بعد الولادة (post‑partum hemorrhage) and موت الجنين (fetal death). This category is also classified as تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم (obstetric cervical tear) and ورم دموي توليدي حوضي (pelvic obstetric hematoma), indicating that cervical tears and pelvic hematomas represent particular subtypes within the spectrum of maternal management complications during the puerperium. Recognizing that نزف ما بعد الولادة, موت الجنين, تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم, and ورم دموي توليدي حوضي are interrelated components of Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is essential for comprehensive assessment and effective care of the mother in the post‑delivery period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 789
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a broad category of maternal conditions that encompasses specific child complications such as نزف ما بعد الولادة (post‑partum hemorrhage) and فشل تحريض المخاض (failure to induce labor). This category is also classified under تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم (obstetric cervical tear) and ورم دموي توليدي حوضي (pelvic obstetric hematoma). Together, these relationships illustrate that postpartum hemorrhage and failure to induce labor are considered part of the larger group of birth‑ and puerperium‑related complications that may involve cervical tears and pelvic hematomas, underscoring their interconnected nature and relevance to the clinical management of the mother.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2656
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a clinical category that encompasses both تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم (obstetric cervical tear) and ورم دموي توليدي حوضي (obstetric pelvic hematoma). This category is also associated with the adverse outcome موت الجنين (fetal death), indicating that cervical tears and pelvic hematomas occurring in the postpartum period can contribute to fetal mortality and require careful management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 761
retrieved_docs.content: a parent category for hemorrhage in pregnancy, and the higher‑level groups “pregnancy and puerperal disorders” and “maternal pregnancy complications” (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم) are also listed as parents of hemorrhage in pregnancy. This hierarchical structure underscores that hemorrhage in pregnancy is a specific disorder within the larger spectrum of gestational abnormalities and maternal obstetric disorders. Overall, the knowledge base connects hemorrhage in pregnancy with numerous specific bleeding scenarios, coagulation disorders, placental pathologies, and other pregnancy‑related complications, emphasizing the importance of recognizing these interrelated conditions for comprehensive maternal care and effective management during the antenatal period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
retrieved_docs.content: maternal and fetal outcomes. It includes postpartum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة) and fetal death (موت الجنين) as child conditions, and it is classified under specific obstetric injuries such as cervical laceration (تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم) and pelvic hematoma (ورم دموي توليدي حوضي). This reflects the heterogeneous nature of postpartum complications, which can range from severe bleeding to tissue injury and fetal loss. The same complications category is also classified as an “Unspecified indication for care or intervention related to labor and delivery, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” underscoring that many of these events are captured under broad, non‑specific labor‑and‑delivery care codes. The “Unspecified indication for care or intervention related to labor and delivery, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” entity has a broader relationship to both the pelvic abnormality group and to other obstetric conditions such as “Unspecified

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2388
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage during pregnancy, including the specific conditions abruptio placenta and placenta previa, are classified together as types of obstetric hemorrhage. This classification groups these distinct pregnancy‑related bleeding disorders, emphasizing their shared relevance to maternal health and guiding clinical assessment and management of hemorrhagic complications in pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical insufficiency, postpartum sepsis, retained placenta or membranes without hemorrhage, prolonged pregnancy, other previous delivery, unspecified early‑pregnancy bleeding, and malignant tumor of the placenta are all classified as members of the “Complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor and delivery (excluding miscarriage and stillbirth) (SMQ).” This SMQ category groups together a range of obstetric conditions that represent significant complications or risk factors during pregnancy, labor, and the postpartum period, emphasizing their importance for clinical assessment, monitoring, and management in maternal‑fetal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 943
retrieved_docs.content: hemorrhage and placental bleeding. Collectively, these interrelated entities and relationships illustrate how various uterine and hemorrhagic conditions are organized under the overarching concepts of pregnancy complications, maternal antenatal events, and broader obstetric disorder categories, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing these interconnected obstetric conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 719
retrieved_docs.content: and hemorrhage in pregnancy (نزف في الحمل), underscoring that these specific conditions are particular examples of pregnancy‑related complications. This network of relationships highlights the interconnection of hemorrhagic events with various maternal, placental, and coagulation disorders, emphasizing the importance of recognizing and managing hemorrhage in pregnancy within comprehensive obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2073
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY encompasses several serious obstetric conditions, specifically hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa. These three entities are classified as hemorrhagic complications of pregnancy, representing major sources of maternal morbidity and mortality. Recognizing that hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa fall under the broader category of pregnancy‑related complications highlights their interrelated nature and underscores the importance of prompt diagnosis and coordinated management in maternal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: organizes general obstetric procedure categories (إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures, Obstetrics incision) and specific clinical actions—ranging from diagnostic sampling and membrane management to fetal stimulation, reduction of multiple gestations, and surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancies—providing a comprehensive framework for coordinating obstetric care across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Bleeding in pregnancy (نزف في الحمل) is a central obstetric disorder that encompasses a wide spectrum of hemorrhagic conditions and related gestational abnormalities. Specific clinical entities classified as children of bleeding in pregnancy include Partial placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage, Total placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage, Parturient hemorrhage associated with afibrinogenemia, Parturient hemorrhage associated with hypofibrinogenemia, Parturient hemorrhage associated with hyperfibrinolysis, bleeding during labor (نزف أثناء الولادة), unspecified early‑pregnancy bleeding (نزف غير محدد في وقت باكر من الحمل), and a vascular tumor behind the placenta (ورم دموي خلف المشيمة). In addition, a placental condition described as “مشيمة منزاحة من دون نزف” (placenta previa without bleeding) is linked to the broader category of bleeding in pregnancy, underscoring that placenta previa can occur with or without hemorrhage but remains relevant to the overall spectrum of pregnancy‑related bleeding disorders. Gestation abnormality is identified as a parent category of bleeding in pregnancy, and the broader group of pregnancy and puerperal disorders also serves as a parent, indicating that obstetric hemorrhage is an integral component of general gestational pathology. Recognizing these interrelationships helps clinicians appreciate the diverse etiologies of obstetric hemorrhage, facilitates accurate diagnosis and risk stratification, and supports targeted management strategies to improve maternal and fetal outcomes.
